combined_corpus.title: Additional evidence for a therapeutic effect of dextromethorphan/quinidine on bulbar motor function in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: A quantitative speech analysis.
authors: Green JR, Allison KM, Cordella C, Richburg BD, Pattee GL, Berry JD, Macklin EA, Pioro EP, Smith RA.
combined_corpus.abstract: A recent double-blind placebo-controlled crossover 70-day trial demonstrated that a fixed combination of dextromethorphan and quinidine (DM/Q) improves speech and swallowing function in most patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. In this study, a subset of participants, many of whom did not substantially improve while on DM/Q, were re-evaluated using computer-based speech analyses and expert clinician ratings of the overall severity of speech impairment. Speech samples were recorded from the subset of 10 patients at four visits made at approximately 30-day intervals. The recordings were analysed by automated computer-based analysis of speech pausing patterns. Severity of speech impairment was rated by three experienced speech-language pathologists using direct magnitude estimation. Scores on patient-reported and clinician-administered scales of bulbar motor involvement were obtained at each visit. The effects of DM/Q were detected on several of the objective speech measures, including total pause duration (s) (Cohen's d = 0.73, 95% confidence interval (CI) -1.70, 0.24), pause time (%) (d = 0.77, 95% CI -1.75, 0.21), and mean speech event duration (s) (d = 0.52, 95% CI -0.44, 1.47), but not on clinician ratings of speech or the speech components of the self-report or clinician-administered scales. These findings suggest that even patients with modest improvement while on DM/Q may experience quantifiable improvements in speech when assessed using sensitive and objective measures. This study provides additional evidence of the positive impact of DM/Q on one or more of the neural systems that control bulbar motor function and production of speech.

raw.abstractText:
Aims

A recent double-blind placebo-controlled crossover 70-day trial demonstrated that a fixed combination of dextromethorphan and quinidine (DM/Q) improves speech and swallowing function in most patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. In this study, a subset of participants, many of whom did not substantially improve while on DM/Q, were re-evaluated using computer-based speech analyses and expert clinician ratings of the overall severity of speech impairment.

Methods

Speech samples were recorded from the subset of 10 patients at four visits made at approximately 30-day intervals. The recordings were analysed by automated computer-based analysis of speech pausing patterns. Severity of speech impairment was rated by three experienced speech-language pathologists using direct magnitude estimation. Scores on patient-reported and clinician-administered scales of bulbar motor involvement were obtained at each visit.

Results

The effects of DM/Q were detected on several of the objective speech measures, including total pause duration (s) (Cohen's d = 0.73, 95% confidence interval (CI) -1.70, 0.24), pause time (%) (d = 0.77, 95% CI -1.75, 0.21), and mean speech event duration (s) (d = 0.52, 95% CI -0.44, 1.47), but not on clinician ratings of speech or the speech components of the self-report or clinician-administered scales.

Conclusions

These findings suggest that even patients with modest improvement while on DM/Q may experience quantifiable improvements in speech when assessed using sensitive and objective measures. This study provides additional evidence of the positive impact of DM/Q on one or more of the neural systems that control bulbar motor function and production of speech.

Severity of speech impairment was rated by three experienced speech-language pathologists using direct magnitude estimation. Scores on patient-reported and clinician-administered scales of bulbar motor involvement were obtained at each visit. The effects of DM/Q were detected on several of the objective speech measures, including total pause duration (s) (Cohen's d = 0.73, 95% confidence interval (CI) -1.70, 0.24), pause time (%) (d = 0.77, 95% CI -1.75, 0.21), and mean speech event duration (s) (d = 0.52, 95% CI -0.44, 1.47), but not on clinician ratings of speech or the speech components of the self-report or clinician-administered scales. These findings suggest that even patients with modest improvement while on DM/Q may experience quantifiable improvements in speech when assessed using sensitive and objective measures. This study provides additional evidence of the positive impact of DM/Q on one or more of the neural systems that control bulbar motor function and production of speech.

combined_corpus.title: Repeatability of Commonly Used Speech and Language Features for Clinical Applications.
authors: Stegmann GM, Hahn S, Liss J, Shefner J, Rutkove SB, Kawabata K, Bhandari S, Shelton K, Duncan CJ, Berisha V.
combined_corpus.abstract: Changes in speech have the potential to provide important information on the diagnosis and progression of various neurological diseases. Many researchers have relied on open-source speech features to develop algorithms for measuring speech changes in clinical populations as they are convenient and easy to use. However, the repeatability of open-source features in the context of neurological diseases has not been studied. We used a longitudinal sample of healthy controls, individuals with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and individuals with suspected frontotemporal dementia, and we evaluated the repeatability of acoustic and language features separately on these 3 data sets. Repeatability was evaluated using intraclass correlation (ICC) and the within-subjects coefficient of variation (WSCV). In 3 sets of tasks, the median ICC were between 0.02 and 0.55, and the median WSCV were between 29 and 79%. Our results demonstrate that the repeatability of speech features extracted using open-source tool kits is low. Researchers should exercise caution when developing digital health models with open-source speech features. We provide a detailed summary of feature-by-feature repeatability results (ICC, WSCV, SE of measurement, limits of agreement for WSCV, and minimal detectable change) in the online supplementary material so that researchers may incorporate repeatability information into the models they develop.

combined_corpus.title: Objectively Monitoring Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis Patient Symptoms During Clinical Trials With Sensors: Observational Study.
authors: Garcia-Gancedo L, Kelly ML, Lavrov A, Parr J, Hart R, Marsden R, Turner MR, Talbot K, Chiwera T, Shaw CE, Al-Chalabi A.
combined_corpus.abstract: Objective symptom monitoring of patients with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) has the potential to provide an important source of information to evaluate the impact of the disease on aspects of real-world functional capacity and activities of daily living in the home setting, providing useful objective outcome measures for clinical trials. This study aimed to investigate the feasibility of a novel digital platform for remote data collection of multiple symptoms-physical activity, heart rate variability (HRV), and digital speech characteristics-in 25 patients with ALS in an observational clinical trial setting to explore the impact of the devices on patients' everyday life and to record tolerability related to the devices and study procedures over 48 weeks. In this exploratory, noncontrolled, nondrug study, patients attended a clinical site visit every 3 months to perform activity reference tasks while wearing a sensor, to conduct digital speech tests and for conventional ALS monitoring. In addition, patients wore the sensor in their daily life for approximately 3 days every month for the duration of the study. The amount and quality of digital speech data captured at the clinical sites were as intended, and there were no significant issues. All the home monitoring sensor data available were propagated through the system and were received as expected. However, the amount and quality of physical activity home monitoring data were lower than anticipated. A total of 3 or more days (or partial days) of data were recorded for 65% of protocol time points, with no data collected for 24% of time points. At baseline, 24 of 25 patients provided data, reduced to 13 of 18 patients at Week 48. Lower-than-expected quality HRV data were obtained, likely because of poor contact between the sensor and the skin. In total, 6 of 25 patients had mild or moderate adverse events (AEs) in the skin and subcutaneous tissue disorders category because of skin irritation caused by the electrode patch. There were no reports of serious AEs or deaths. Most patients found the sensor comfortable, with no or minimal impact on daily activities. The platform can measure physical activity in patients with ALS in their home environment; patients used the equipment successfully, and it was generally well tolerated. The quantity of home monitoring physical activity data was lower than expected, although it was sufficient to allow investigation of novel physical activity end points. Good-quality in-clinic speech data were successfully captured for analysis. Future studies using objective patient monitoring approaches, combined with the most current technological advances, may be useful to elucidate novel digital biomarkers of disease progression.

raw.abstractText:
Background

Objective symptom monitoring of patients with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) has the potential to provide an important source of information to evaluate the impact of the disease on aspects of real-world functional capacity and activities of daily living in the home setting, providing useful objective outcome measures for clinical trials.

Objective

This study aimed to investigate the feasibility of a novel digital platform for remote data collection of multiple symptoms-physical activity, heart rate variability (HRV), and digital speech characteristics-in 25 patients with ALS in an observational clinical trial setting to explore the impact of the devices on patients' everyday life and to record tolerability related to the devices and study procedures over 48 weeks.

Methods

In this exploratory, noncontrolled, nondrug study, patients attended a clinical site visit every 3 months to perform activity reference tasks while wearing a sensor, to conduct digital speech tests and for conventional ALS monitoring. In addition, patients wore the sensor in their daily life for approximately 3 days every month for the duration of the study.

Results

The amount and quality of digital speech data captured at the clinical sites were as intended, and there were no significant issues. All the home monitoring sensor data available were propagated through the system and were received as expected. However, the amount and quality of physical activity home monitoring data were lower than anticipated. A total of 3 or more days (or partial days) of data were recorded for 65% of protocol time points, with no data collected for 24% of time points. At baseline, 24 of 25 patients provided data, reduced to 13 of 18 patients at Week 48. Lower-than-expected quality HRV data were obtained, likely because of poor contact between the sensor and the skin. In total, 6 of 25 patients had mild or moderate adverse events (AEs) in the skin and subcutaneous tissue disorders category because of skin irritation caused by the electrode patch. There were no reports of serious AEs or deaths. Most patients found the sensor comfortable, with no or minimal impact on daily activities.

Conclusions

The platform can measure physical activity in patients with ALS in their home environment; patients used the equipment successfully, and it was generally well tolerated. The quantity of home monitoring physical activity data was lower than expected, although it was sufficient to allow investigation of novel physical activity end points. Good-quality in-clinic speech data were successfully captured for analysis. Future studies using objective patient monitoring approaches, combined with the most current technological advances, may be useful to elucidate novel digital biomarkers of disease progression.

combined_corpus.title: Detection of Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) via Acoustic Analysis
authors: Norel R, Pietrowicz M, Agurto C, Rishoni S, Cecchi G.
combined_corpus.abstract:
ABSTRACT

ALS is a fatal neurodegenerative disease with no cure. Experts typically measure disease progression via the ALSFRS-R score, which includes measurements of various abilities known to decline. We propose instead the use of speech analysis as a proxy for ALS progression. This technique enables 1) frequent non-invasive, inexpensive, longitudinal analysis, 2) analysis of data recorded in the wild, and 3) creation of an extensive ALS databank for future analysis. Patients and trained medical professionals need not be co-located, enabling more frequent monitoring of more patients from the convenience of their own homes. The goals of this study are the identification of acoustic speech features in naturalistic contexts which characterize disease progression and development of machine models which can recognize the presence and severity of the disease. We evaluated subjects from the Prize4Life Israel dataset, using a variety of frequency, spectral, and voice quality features. The dataset was generated using the ALS Mobile Analyzer, a cell-phone app that collects data regarding disease progress using a self-reported ALSFRS-R questionnaire and several active tasks that measure speech and motor skills. Classification via leave-five-subjects-out cross-validation resulted in an accuracy rate of 79% (61% chance) for males and 83% (52% chance) for females.

combined_corpus.title: Design and results of a smartphone-based digital phenotyping study to quantify ALS progression.
authors: Berry JD, Paganoni S, Carlson K, Burke K, Weber H, Staples P, Salinas J, Chan J, Green JR, Connaghan K, Barback J, Onnela JP.
combined_corpus.journal: Annals of clinical and translational neurology
combined_corpus.abstract: The amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) trial outcome measures are clinic based. Active and passive smartphone data can provide important longitudinal information about ALS progression outside the clinic. We used Beiwe, a research platform for smartphone-based digital phenotyping, to collect active (self-report ALSFRS-R surveys and speech recordings) and passive (phone sensors and logs) data from patients with ALS for approximately 24 weeks. In clinics, at baseline and every 3 months, we collected vital capacity, ALSFRS-R, and ALS-CBS at enrollment, week 12, and week 24. We also collected ALSFRS-R by telephone at week 6. Baseline in-clinic ALSFRS-R and smartphone self-report correlation was 0.93 (P < 0.001). ALSFRS-R slopes were equivalent and within-subject standard deviation was smaller for smartphone-based self-report (0.26 vs. 0.56). Use of Beiwe afforded weekly collection of speech samples amenable to a variety of analyses, and we found mean pause time to increase by 0.02 sec per month across the sample. Smartphone-based digital phenotyping in people with ALS is feasible and informative. Self-administered smartphone ALSFRS-R scores correlate highly with clinic-based ALSFRS-R scores, have low variability, and could be used in clinical trials. More research is required to fully analyze speech recordings and passive data, and to identify optimal digital markers for use in future ALS clinical trials.

raw.abstractText:
Objective

The amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) trial outcome measures are clinic based. Active and passive smartphone data can provide important longitudinal information about ALS progression outside the clinic.

Methods

We used Beiwe, a research platform for smartphone-based digital phenotyping, to collect active (self-report ALSFRS-R surveys and speech recordings) and passive (phone sensors and logs) data from patients with ALS for approximately 24 weeks. In clinics, at baseline and every 3 months, we collected vital capacity, ALSFRS-R, and ALS-CBS at enrollment, week 12, and week 24. We also collected ALSFRS-R by telephone at week 6.

Results

Baseline in-clinic ALSFRS-R and smartphone self-report correlation was 0.93 (P < 0.001). ALSFRS-R slopes were equivalent and within-subject standard deviation was smaller for smartphone-based self-report (0.26 vs. 0.56). Use of Beiwe afforded weekly collection of speech samples amenable to a variety of analyses, and we found mean pause time to increase by 0.02 sec per month across the sample.

Interpretation

Smartphone-based digital phenotyping in people with ALS is feasible and informative. Self-administered smartphone ALSFRS-R scores correlate highly with clinic-based ALSFRS-R scores, have low variability, and could be used in clinical trials. More research is required to fully analyze speech recordings and passive data, and to identify optimal digital markers for use in future ALS clinical trials.

combined_corpus.title: Speech-Based Digital Endpoints Track ALS Progression and Align with Standard Clinical Outcomes: Evidence from the VRG50635 Trial
authors: Neumann M, Kothare H, Bartlett M, Roesler O, Suendermann-Oeft C, Hosamath A, Arbatti L, Pautler D, Suendermann-Oeft D, Cadavid D, Scannevin RH, Hoffmann I, Tarachandani A, Haley T, Raines S, Damme PV, Tienari P, Solje E, Jokela M, Genge A, O'Connell C, Eijk RPv, Berg LHvd, Ramanarayanan V.
combined_corpus.abstract: We report on the clinical validity of speech-based digital endpoints measured during a Phase 1b study of VRG50635 in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). Fifty-four participants with ALS were enrolled and participated in an 8-week pretreatment run-in, followed by three 8-week dosing periods and an 8-week follow-up. They completed a speech assessment every two weeks in the clinic or at home. We observed moderate to high correlations between digital measures of speech timing and articulatory motor function and traditional clinical outcome measures -- such as the ALS Functional Rating Scale-Revised, slow vital capacity and plasma neurofilament light chain. Furthermore, speech measures can show functional decline before the ALSFRS-R does, while also capturing differences between participants with bulbar symptoms and those without. The results support the feasibility, utility, and validity of digital speech endpoints to study disease impact in ALS clinical trials.

combined_corpus.title: A Machine-Learning Based Objective Measure for ALS Disease Severity
authors: Vieira FG, Venugopalan S, Premasiri AS, McNally M, Jansen A, McCloskey K, Brenner MP, Perrin S.
combined_corpus.abstract:
ABSTRACT

Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) disease severity is usually measured using the subjective, questionnaire-based revised ALS Functional Rating Scale (ALSFRS-R). An objective measure for tracking disease severity and progression would be a powerful tool for evaluating real-world drug effectiveness, efficacy in clinical trials, as well as identifying participants for cohort studies. Here we develop a machine learning (ML) based objective measure for ALS disease severity, based on voice samples and accelerometer measurements from a longitudinal dataset collected by the ALS Therapy Development Institute (ALS-TDI) over four years. 584 people living with ALS consented and carried out prescribed speaking and limb-based tasks. 542 participants contributed 5814 voice recordings, and 350 contributed 13009 accelerometer samples, while simultaneously measuring ALSFRS-R scores. Using the data from 475 participants, we trained machine learning (ML) models to predict bulbar-related FRS scores from speech, and limb-related scores from accelerometer samples. On the test set (n=109 participants) the voice models achieved a multiclass AUC of 0.86 (95% CI, 0.85-0.88) on speech FRS prediction, whereas the accelerometer models achieved a median multiclass AUC of 0.73 on 6 limb-related functions. Further, the correlation (and inverse correlations) across different bulbar and limb functions observed in the self-reported ALSFRS-R scores are preserved in the ML-derived scores. Next, we used these models and self-reported ALSFRS-R scores to evaluate the real-world effects of edaravone, a drug recently approved for use in ALS. In the cohort of 54 test participants who received edaravone as part of their usual care, the ML-derived objective scores were consistent with the self-reported ALSFRS-R scores, and both showed highly variable outcomes from edaravone treatment from person to person. At the individual level, the continuous ML-derived score is able to capture a gradual change that is absent in the integer ALSFRS-R scores. This demonstrates the value of these tools for assessing both disease severity and potentially drug effects.

raw.authorString: Vieira FG, Venugopalan S, Premasiri AS, McNally M, Jansen A, McCloskey K, Brenner MP, Perrin S.

combined_corpus.title: Remote Assessment for ALS using Multimodal Dialog Agents: Data Quality, Feasibility and Task Compliance.
authors: Richter V, Neumann M, Green JR, Richburg B, Roesler O, Kothare H, Ramanarayanan V.
combined_corpus.abstract: We investigate the feasibility, task compliance and audiovisual data quality of a multimodal dialog-based solution for remote assessment of Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). 53 people with ALS and 52 healthy controls interacted with Tina, a cloud-based conversational agent, in performing speech tasks designed to probe various aspects of motor speech function while their audio and video was recorded. We rated a total of 250 recordings for audio/video quality and participant task compliance, along with the relative frequency of different issues observed. We observed excellent compliance (98%) and audio (95.2%) and visual quality rates (84.8%), resulting in an overall yield of 80.8% recordings that were both compliant and of high quality. Furthermore, recording quality and compliance were not affected by level of speech severity and did not differ significantly across end devices. These findings support the utility of dialog systems for remote monitoring of speech in ALS.

raw.title: Remote Assessment for ALS using Multimodal Dialog Agents: Data Quality, Feasibility and Task Compliance.

combined_corpus.title: Use of Beiwe Smartphone App to Identify and Track Speech Decline in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)
combined_corpus.abstract: The capacity for smartphones to remotely capture speech data affords significant clinical and research opportunities for degenerative neurologic diseases such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). Longitudinal data may inform ALS disease prognosis, facilitate timely intervention, and document response to treatment [1]. A recent study established the feasibility of the Beiwe smartphone-based digital phenotyping to track the clinical progression of ALS across multiple domains [2]. The current investigation extends this work to address the utility of Beiwe to identify and track speech decline in ALS. Twelve participants with ALS used the Beiwe app weekly to record reading passages and self-report (ALSFRS-R) ratings of bulbar (speech) function. Speaking rate and pause variables were automatically extracted from recordings offline [3]. Speech function measures at baseline were significantly different for participants with and without bulbar symptoms. In addition, the rate of decline of all measured speech functions was greater for participants with bulbar symptoms. The successful use of Beiwe for speech function analysis suggests that smartphone-based capture of speech has potential for diagnostic screening and disease progress monitoring in ALS. Further large sample investigation across a comprehensive set of speech variables is warranted.

raw.title: Use of Beiwe Smartphone App to Identify and Track Speech Decline in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)

combined_corpus.title: PROSA—a multicenter prospective observational study to develop low-burden digital speech biomarkers in ALS and FTD
combined_corpus.abstract: Objective: There is a need for novel biomarkers that can indicate disease state, project disease progression, or assess response to treatment for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and associated neurodegenerative diseases such as frontotemporal dementia (FTD). Digital biomarkers are especially promising as they can be collected non-invasively and at low burden for patients. Speech biomarkers have the potential to objectively measure cognitive, motor as well as respiratory symptoms at low-cost and in a remote fashion using widely available technology such as telephone calls. Methods: The PROSA study aims to develop and evaluate low-burden frequent prognostic digital speech biomarkers. The main goal is to create a single, easy-to-perform battery that serves as a valid and reliable proxy for cognitive, respiratory, and motor domains in ALS and FTD. The study will be a multicenter 12-months observational study aiming to include 75 ALS and 75 FTD patients as well as 50 healthy controls and build on three established longitudinal cohorts: DANCER, DESCRIBE-ALS and DESCRIBE-FTD. In addition to the extensive clinical phenotyping in DESCRIBE, PROSA collects a comprehensive speech protocol in fully remote and automated fashion over the telephone at four time points. This longitudinal speech data, together with gold standard measures, will allow advanced speech analysis using artificial intelligence for the development of speech-based phenotypes of ALS and FTD patients measuring cognitive, motor and respiratory symptoms. Conclusion: Speech-based phenotypes can be used to develop diagnostic and prognostic models predicting clinical change. Results are expected to have implications for future clinical trial stratification as well as supporting innovative trial designs in ALS and FTD.

raw.title: PROSA—a multicenter prospective observational study to develop low-burden digital speech biomarkers in ALS and FTD
raw.venue: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Frontotemporal Degeneration

combined_corpus.title: Multimodal Digital Biomarkers for Longitudinal Tracking of Speech Impairment Severity in ALS: An Investigation of Clinically Important Differences
combined_corpus.abstract: Speech biomarkers have shown promise for the remote assessment of ALS. However, to demonstrate clinical utility at tracking longitudinal progress of the disease, one needs to understand how well these biomarkers capture changes that are ‘clin-ically meaningful’, a concept that is not always clearly defined. Therefore, this paper defines and explores multiple methods of computing minimal clinically important difference (MCID) us-ing ratings of speech impairment severity and listener effort as clinical anchors. We analyze how these methods impact the estimated responsiveness of various metrics collected from 125 ALS patients via a multimodal dialog based remote assessment platform. We find that select biomarkers are more responsive than the clinical standard ALSFRS-R across the board at tracking clinically meaningful changes related to speech severity. We further discuss advantages and disadvantages of different MCID computation methods for assessing ALS disease progression.

raw.title: Multimodal Digital Biomarkers for Longitudinal Tracking of Speech Impairment Severity in ALS: An Investigation of Clinically Important Differences

raw.abstract: Speech biomarkers have shown promise for the remote assessment of ALS. However, to demonstrate clinical utility at tracking longitudinal progress of the disease, one needs to understand how well these biomarkers capture changes that are ‘clin-ically meaningful’, a concept that is not always clearly defined. Therefore, this paper defines and explores multiple methods of computing minimal clinically important difference (MCID) us-ing ratings of speech impairment severity and listener effort as clinical anchors. We analyze how these methods impact the estimated responsiveness of various metrics collected from 125 ALS patients via a multimodal dialog based remote assessment platform. We find that select biomarkers are more responsive than the clinical standard ALSFRS-R across the board at tracking clinically meaningful changes related to speech severity. We further discuss advantages and disadvantages of different MCID computation methods for assessing ALS disease progression.

combined_corpus.title: Development and Validation of a Wav2Vec 2.0-Based Cross-Language Methodology for Measurement of Articulatory Precision
combined_corpus.abstract: Degraded speech intelligibility due to dysarthrias can result from changes inherent to diseases such as Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). Tracking this degradation longitudinally can highlight treatment effects. Various approaches have been developed to automatically measure speech intelligibility, often focusing on output from automatic speech recognition systems. In this paper, we extract phonetic outputs from the wav2vec 2.0 model and compare them to expected phonetic outputs across 12 languages to derive Articulatory Precision (ArtP). The strongest correlations between ArtP and Azure Pronunciation Assessment were 0.93 for English, 0.85 in German, and 0.66 for Swedish. We additionally find that ArtP has a correlation of 0.77 with the ALSFRS-R speech subscore and is sensitive to perceptual measures of speech intelligibility. The measure shows promise as a reliable and clinically relevant tool that can be used in multiple languages and disorders to assess speech intelligibility.

raw.title: Development and Validation of a Wav2Vec 2.0-Based Cross-Language Methodology for Measurement of Articulatory Precision

raw.abstract: Degraded speech intelligibility due to dysarthrias can result from changes inherent to diseases such as Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). Tracking this degradation longitudinally can highlight treatment effects. Various approaches have been developed to automatically measure speech intelligibility, often focusing on output from automatic speech recognition systems. In this paper, we extract phonetic outputs from the wav2vec 2.0 model and compare them to expected phonetic outputs across 12 languages to derive Articulatory Precision (ArtP). The strongest correlations between ArtP and Azure Pronunciation Assessment were 0.93 for English, 0.85 in German, and 0.66 for Swedish. We additionally find that ArtP has a correlation of 0.77 with the ALSFRS-R speech subscore and is sensitive to perceptual measures of speech intelligibility. The measure shows promise as a reliable and clinically relevant tool that can be used in multiple languages and disorders to assess speech intelligibility.

combined_devices.device: Microsoft Kinect (3D Depth Sensor) for Reachable Workspace
combined_devices.wear_location: Not worn — external depth sensor positioned facing the patient
combined_devices.limitations: Microsoft Kinect is commercially discontinued (production ceased ~2017); all ALS-specific validation studies used small samples (n=10 ALS per study); no MID/MICD established for RSA in ALS; validation limited to clinic-based supervised setting; no remote/home use evidence; methodology validated but current commercial successor device not identified or validated in ALS in this corpus

combined_devices.device: Inertial Measurement Unit (body-fixed, unspecified brand) for Head-Neck and Torso ROM
combined_devices.wear_location: Forehead + sternum (head-neck ROM); chest-mounted (EATUG torso kinematics)
measures_supported: Head-neck angular velocity (flexion-extension, lateral bending, axial rotation)
combined_devices.limitations: No specific commercial IMU device named in any study — all use generic/unbranded sensors; cross-sectional discrimination only (ALS vs HC or ALS vs PD), no longitudinal change detection; pilot-level sample sizes (n=13 ALS, n=20 ALS); EATUG study available only as conference abstract (abstract-only extraction — methods detail not visible); no trial endpoint use; no remote/home validation; head-neck sub-construct correlation with ALSFRS-R and FVC shown but not validated as standalone endpoint

All validation in clinic/trial-site settings only (no home/remote deployment validated); industry-sponsored or industry-associated studies (no fully independent replication); sample sizes 28–45; no MID/MICD established; algorithm is proprietary with no published SOP; regulatory clearance status not identified; criterion 7 (validation setting) is Weak — no unsupervised or remote-use validation for this platform in the corpus

Feasibility study only (N=10) with no quantitative validation metrics (no ICC, no correlation, no accuracy reported vs. VOG reference); no longitudinal data; no trial endpoint use; no regulatory clearance; manufacturer/developer not clearly identified in corpus; study demonstrates usability and patient preference but not measurement validity

combined_devices.manufacturer: Multiple (e.g., microFET, Citec — specific brand rarely named in corpus)
combined_devices.regulatory_clearance: FDA 510(k) cleared (class of device); specific clearance numbers not stated in corpus
combined_devices.wear_location: Handheld — applied against limb by examiner
combined_devices.limitations: Examiner-dependent with ceiling effects when patient strength exceeds examiner's (PMID:33355879); primarily clinic-based requiring trained examiner; inter-examiner variability across sites not systematically quantified in this corpus; no established MID/MICD for ALS identified in corpus

combined_devices.device: ATLIS (Accurate Test of Limb Isometric Strength)
combined_devices.manufacturer: Cedars-Sinai Medical Center / research consortium (no commercial manufacturer named)
combined_devices.wear_location: Fixed wireless load cell — patient pushes against fixed frame
combined_devices.limitations: Requires fixed frame equipment (clinic-based only); no FDA clearance identified; not yet used as primary endpoint in Phase 3; limited to 3 key publications in corpus though one (PMID:28120413) is a well-powered multi-site longitudinal study

combined_devices.manufacturer: Multiple (e.g., Jamar — specific brand not named in corpus)
combined_devices.regulatory_clearance: FDA 510(k) cleared (class of device); specific number not stated
combined_devices.limitations: Measures only hand/grip function — single sub-construct of overall muscle function; strong correlation with ALSFRS-R fine motor (rs=0.740) but does not capture lower-extremity or proximal strength; may not be meaningful for patients with bulbar-onset or lower-limb-predominant ALS

combined_devices.manufacturer: Unknown (described as 'newly developed' by Dutch academic group — UMC Utrecht implied)
combined_devices.wear_location: Fixed to furniture/frame — patient pushes against fixed sensor at home
combined_devices.limitations: Only 2 publications (N=18 and N=45); single academic group; currently measures only knee extensors; no regulatory clearance; no use as trial endpoint; needs multi-muscle-group expansion and larger longitudinal cohort. However: ICC 0.97, SEM 5.8%, eliminates examiner ceiling effects, and demonstrated monthly decline detection at home — strong candidate for advancement

combined_devices.manufacturer: Unknown (Skulpt implied in at-home studies but not explicitly named in corpus)
combined_devices.wear_location: Surface electrode array applied to skin over target muscle
measures_supported: Muscle impedance parameters (reactance, resistance, phase angle)
combined_devices.limitations: Corpus provides only indirect evidence: mentioned in systematic review (PMID:35945397, 1 study), used as one tool in at-home study designs (PMID:32515889, PMID:30486680), and one Phase 1 trial (NCT02917681). No dedicated ALS validation paper with performance metrics in this corpus. Primary validation literature likely exists outside corpus.

combined_devices.wear_location: Wrist or waist (not specified in corpus)
combined_devices.limitations: Measures physical activity as an indirect proxy for muscle function rather than direct strength; systematic review (PMID:35945397) notes 9 accelerometer studies but HIGH risk of bias across all and need for larger longitudinal validation. Specific devices not named in corpus. Indirect relationship to muscle function COI — more relevant to physical activity COI.

combined_devices.manufacturer: Custom academic development (Italian university consortium implied)
combined_devices.wear_location: Surface electrodes on target muscle + dynamometer interface
combined_devices.limitations: Single conference abstract/paper; N=8 ALS patients; usability study only (VAS/SUS scores) without clinical validation metrics; no longitudinal data; no trial use; no commercial availability identified; custom academic prototype

combined_devices.device: Automated Speech Analytics Platform (FDA Breakthrough Device Designation for ALS Monitoring)
combined_devices.regulatory_clearance: FDA Breakthrough Device Designation for monitoring ALS (PMID:38838248, PMID:38932502)
combined_devices.limitations: Manufacturer not explicitly named in corpus abstracts; author independence scored Moderate due to some papers clearly from platform developers; no Phase 3 primary endpoint trial yet completed; cross-language validation emerging but limited (DOI:10.21437/interspeech.2025-2162 shows correlations of 0.66-0.93 across languages)

ALS-specific sample size limited (15 ALS in differentiation study); validation primarily cross-sectional across multiple diseases rather than ALS-specific longitudinal tracking; no trial endpoint use in ALS found in corpus; longitudinal sensitivity not formally demonstrated in ALS population specifically; no MID/MCID reported

measures_supported: 53 acoustic features covering articulation, phonation, prosody, resonance, and timing
combined_devices.limitations: Analytical and clinical validity demonstrated (Spearman rho >0.70 with clinical measures and lab-standard pipeline) but no formal trial endpoint use; ALS severity stratification achieved (AUROC 0.85 ALS vs controls; 0.78 early ALS vs controls) but formal longitudinal change-score analysis not reported in corpus; no MID/MCID; no regulatory submission reported; some repeatability concerns for open-source features flagged in related work (DOI:10.1159/000511671)

combined_devices.manufacturer: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health / Onnela Lab
combined_devices.limitations: Research platform rather than commercial endpoint solution; feasibility demonstrated (24-week tracking, smartphone ALSFRS-R correlation 0.93 with clinic) but speech analysis limited to basic pause/rate features without validated composite measure; single academic group primarily; no regulatory pathway; mean pause time increased 0.02 sec/month but no MDC/MCID established; small speech-specific sample (12 participants in speech analysis sub-study)

Predicts ALSFRS-R subscores (proxy endpoint) rather than measuring speech directly; multiclass AUC 0.86 for speech FRS prediction is strong but the endpoint is still a predicted questionnaire score; single organization for data collection and model development; used to evaluate real-world edaravone effects but not as formal trial endpoint; no regulatory pathway; non-profit research infrastructure rather than commercial platform positioned for trial deployment

Single publication describing database creation (102 ALS, 51 controls); cross-sectional discrimination between dysarthria severity levels demonstrated (F0 SD excellent ability) but no longitudinal tracking, no trial use, no concurrent validity against gold-standard clinical measures beyond severity grouping; primarily a data resource rather than a validated endpoint platform

measures_supported: syllable count from oral diadochokinesis tasks (/pa/, /pa-ta-ka/, /oo-ee/)
combined_devices.limitations: Very small sample (11 bulbar-onset ALS patients); single study; mean accuracy 90.23% for syllable counting but no clinical validation against gold-standard outcomes; no longitudinal data; no trial use; high bias due to small sample and single-site design; positioned as telemonitoring proof-of-concept rather than validated endpoint

combined_devices.device: Smartphone-Based Tongue Movement Tracker (U-Net++ Deep Learning)
combined_devices.limitations: Single cross-sectional study (37 ALS, 20 controls); novel video-based approach (tongue movement rather than acoustic); significant group differences (p<0.001) and association with dysarthria (p=0.003) and regression model R²=0.43; but no longitudinal data, no trial use, no comparison to established speech measures; promising early-stage tool requiring longitudinal validation

combined_gaps.description: Microsoft Kinect (primary validated sensor for RWS in ALS) is commercially discontinued. No successor 3D depth sensor has been validated for RWS measurement in ALS within this corpus. The RWS methodology is sensor-agnostic but current implementation pathway is undefined.
combined_gaps.action: Identify current-generation depth sensors (e.g., Intel RealSense, Azure Kinect DK successor, LiDAR-based) or motion capture systems implementing RWS/RSA; confirm algorithmic equivalence and conduct bridging validation in ALS cohort

combined_gaps.description: No Minimal Important Difference (MID) or Minimal Clinically Important Difference (MCID) established for RWS RSA in ALS. Cannot determine sample size for a trial powered to detect meaningful change.
combined_gaps.action: Anchor-based MID study linking RSA change to ALSFRS-R meaningful change threshold or patient-reported meaningful decline; distribution-based MID from existing longitudinal dataset

combined_gaps.description: No use of RWS/RSA as a pre-specified primary or secondary endpoint in any interventional RCT in ALS. All evidence is observational or exploratory.
combined_gaps.action: Search for ongoing or planned ALS trials specifying RWS as endpoint; consider inclusion as exploratory endpoint in next Phase 2 to generate interventional sensitivity-to-change data

combined_gaps.description: All ALS-specific RWS validation studies have small samples (n=10 ALS). Insufficient for reliable SEM, test-retest reliability, or subgroup analysis by disease phenotype/severity.
combined_gaps.action: Larger multi-site validation study (n≥50 ALS) with test-retest at short interval and longitudinal follow-up; stratification by ALS phenotype (limb-onset vs bulbar) and baseline ALSFRS-R

combined_gaps.description: No specific commercial IMU device identified or validated for head-neck ROM measurement in ALS. All studies used unnamed research-grade sensors. Cannot recommend a specific procurable device for cervical ROM assessment.
combined_gaps.action: Identify commercial IMU platforms (e.g., Xsens, APDM/Clario, Movella) and assess whether head-neck ROM protocols can be implemented; seek validation data in ALS or adjacent NMD

combined_gaps.description: No FDA or EMA endpoint qualification or guidance document identified supporting RWS/RSA as an acceptable clinical outcome assessment in ALS trials. The COA status is asserted in peer-reviewed literature but not in regulatory record.
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA Drug Development Tool qualification database and EMA qualification opinions for RWS/RSA; if absent, assess pathway for COA qualification submission leveraging existing validation data from NMD studies

combined_gaps.description: No remote or home-based validation of RWS measurement in ALS. All studies conducted in supervised clinic settings. Review paper mentions future remote monitoring potential but no evidence of implementation.
combined_gaps.action: Pilot study of home-based RWS assessment using portable depth sensor or wearable motion capture; assess feasibility, data quality, and comparison to clinic measurement

combined_gaps.description: Head-neck ROM characterization via IMU has no longitudinal data in ALS. Cross-sectional discrimination demonstrated but sensitivity to change over time is unknown.
combined_gaps.action: Longitudinal observational study (≥6 months, ≥3 visits) tracking head-neck IMU metrics in ALS patients with cervical weakness

combined_gaps.description: No MID or MICD established for any oculometric measure in ALS; power calculation in PMID:38975625 estimates sample size for 40% reduction but does not define a clinically meaningful threshold for within-patient change
combined_gaps.action: Anchor trial or dedicated psychometric study needed to establish MID for SWJ frequency or other lead oculometric parameter before any secondary/primary endpoint recommendation

combined_gaps.description: No FDA or EMA guidance document identified that names oculomotor measures as an endpoint of interest in ALS or motor neuron disease; no regulatory qualification or Letter of Support identified in corpus
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA DDT qualification database and EMA qualification advice opinions for oculomotor/eye-tracking biomarkers; if absent, a Biomarker Qualification Plan or pre-submission meeting would be prerequisite to non-exploratory use

combined_gaps.description: NCT05407428 (N=45, completed) results not yet published in peer-reviewed form; study outcomes include correlation data and prediction models that could substantially strengthen or weaken the evidence base
combined_gaps.action: Contact NeuraLight or PI for publication timeline; check conference proceedings for interim results

combined_gaps.description: No independent (non-NeuraLight-associated) validation of the NeuraLight platform; all clinical papers involve the platform in trials where NeuraLight is sponsor or collaborator
combined_gaps.action: Identify whether any fully independent academic group has used or validated the NeuraLight platform; if not, this is a diligence question for the vendor

combined_gaps.description: No home or remote-use validation data for NeuraLight platform in ALS; all studies conducted in clinic/trial-site under supervision. EyePhone demonstrates home-use feasibility but not measurement validity
combined_gaps.action: Request from NeuraLight any remote/unsupervised deployment data or planned studies; determine whether the platform requires trained operator or can be self-administered

combined_gaps.description: Test-retest reliability (ICC), standard error of measurement (SEM), and within-session/between-session variability not reported for any oculometric measure in this corpus
combined_gaps.action: Request psychometric properties from NeuraLight; search for reliability studies in healthy volunteers or other neurological populations that used the same platform

combined_gaps.description: Universe likely incomplete: corpus contains only 4 records and 2 devices; other commercial eye-tracking platforms with potential ALS applicability (e.g., Tobii, SR Research EyeLink, nPoint) are not represented — recall gap due to limited corpus size
combined_gaps.action: Expand retrieval to cover commercial eye-tracking hardware validated in neurological populations; run device-class recovery search for 'eye tracking AND ALS' and 'oculomotor AND neurodegenerative'

combined_gaps.description: NeuraLight algorithm version, processing pipeline, and output standardization are proprietary and undisclosed; no published SOP for measure extraction
combined_gaps.action: Request algorithm version documentation and data-output specifications from vendor for reproducibility assessment

combined_gaps.description: No established Minimal Important Difference (MID) or Minimal Clinically Important Difference (MICD) for hand-held dynamometry or ATLIS in ALS identified in this corpus. Without MID, sample size calculations for a primary endpoint trial cannot be anchored to clinically meaningful change.
combined_gaps.action: Conduct anchor-based MID study linking dynamometry change to patient-meaningful functional milestones (e.g., loss of specific ALSFRS-R functions), or identify existing MID literature outside this corpus.

combined_gaps.description: ATLIS has not been used as a primary or secondary endpoint in a Phase 3 RCT despite evidence of superior sensitivity over ALSFRS-R and VC (sample size reduction ~33%). Phase 2 use only (NCT03127514).
combined_gaps.action: Identify whether any upcoming Phase 3 ALS trial lists ATLIS as primary/secondary; if not, consider proposing its inclusion based on sensitivity data from PMID:28120413.

combined_gaps.description: Specific HHD manufacturer/model is rarely reported in trial publications. Without model-level identification, cross-study comparability and device-specific regulatory positioning cannot be confirmed.
combined_gaps.action: Request methods supplements or contact trial sites to identify specific HHD models (microFET2, Citec CT3002, etc.) used in key Phase 3 trials.

combined_gaps.description: Portable Fixed Dynamometer (PFD) currently validated only for knee extensors in N=18 (home) and N=45 (clinic). Multi-muscle-group expansion and larger sample needed for trial-readiness.
combined_gaps.action: Monitor for publications from the Dutch PFD group expanding to upper-extremity and multi-muscle testing; consider pilot collaboration for broader validation.

combined_gaps.description: No head-to-head comparison of ATLIS vs. HHD vs. PFD measuring the same patients longitudinally identified in this corpus. Cannot determine relative sensitivity or concordance across devices.
combined_gaps.action: Search for comparative studies outside corpus; if none exist, this represents a study design gap to address in a methods trial.

combined_gaps.description: No FDA or EMA guidance document specifically endorsing dynamometry as an acceptable primary endpoint in ALS identified in this corpus. Current use is as secondary endpoint only.
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA guidance documents and advisory committee transcripts for ALS endpoint guidance mentioning muscle strength/dynamometry; check Clinical Outcome Assessment Qualification Program submissions.

combined_gaps.description: EIM device-specific validation in ALS is inadequately represented in this corpus (only indirect mentions). Primary validation literature likely exists but was not retrieved.
combined_gaps.action: Conduct targeted literature search for EIM validation in ALS (Rutkove et al. body of work); this is likely a corpus retrieval gap rather than an evidence gap.

combined_gaps.description: Systematic review (PMID:35945397) identifies HIGH risk of bias across all 20 included digital motor technology studies and notes that quality assessment information was frequently unclear. Field-level evidence quality concern.
combined_gaps.action: When evaluating novel digital devices for muscle function, require pre-registered analysis plans and multi-site designs to address this systemic bias concern.

combined_gaps.description: Home-based dynamometry (PFD, grip dynamometer) demonstrates feasibility and reliability but has not been used as a registered trial endpoint. The transition from feasibility to regulatory-grade endpoint use is untested.
combined_gaps.action: Identify trials planning home-based strength as an endpoint; evaluate whether PFD reliability data (ICC 0.97, SEM 5.8%) meets regulatory threshold for unsupervised outcome assessment.

combined_gaps.description: ATLIS validation study (PMID:28120413) shows prefunctional detection in participants with normal ALSFRS-R (12-32% below expected strength). The specific threshold for prefunctional detection and its relationship to trial enrichment is not characterized.
combined_gaps.action: Request individual-level ATLIS data from NCT02374606 or contact Cedars-Sinai group regarding prefunctional detection characteristics for trial enrichment strategy.

combined_gaps.description: No formal FDA/EMA guidance document for speech-based digital endpoints in ALS clinical trials; FDA BDD designation exists but pathway to primary endpoint acceptance undefined
combined_gaps.action: Monitor FDA Digital Health Center of Excellence communications; request pre-submission meeting if primary endpoint use intended; review any formal feedback from BDD review process

combined_gaps.description: No completed Phase 3 trial with a digital speech measure as primary endpoint in ALS; VRG50635 Phase 1b used speech as secondary/exploratory only
combined_gaps.action: Track ongoing and planned Phase 2/3 ALS trials for speech endpoint inclusion; evaluate whether speech can serve as primary in bulbar-enriched sub-studies

combined_gaps.description: MID/MCID formally established only for articulatory precision; other promising measures (timing alignment, multimodal indexes, listener effort) lack published MCID in peer-reviewed literature
combined_gaps.action: Prioritize MCID anchoring studies for timing alignment and automated listener effort using clinician-rated anchors; DOI:10.21437/interspeech.2024-1318 explores methods but peer-reviewed publication needed

combined_gaps.description: Open-source acoustic features demonstrate poor repeatability (median ICC 0.02-0.55, WSCV 29-79%); platforms using custom validated measures may not be affected but feature-level repeatability should be verified per platform
combined_gaps.action: Request per-feature ICC and WSCV data from any platform under consideration; prioritize platforms with published repeatability data (articulatory precision ICC reported; Wav2DDK test-retest r=0.95)

combined_gaps.description: Platform manufacturer for FDA BDD articulatory precision measure not explicitly stated in corpus abstracts; identification gap limits vendor-engagement planning
combined_gaps.action: Cross-reference FDA BDD database (Breakthrough Devices Program) for ALS speech monitoring designation to confirm manufacturer identity

combined_gaps.description: No head-to-head comparison between commercial platforms (FDA BDD platform vs. ki elements vs. Winterlight Labs) on same dataset in ALS
combined_gaps.action: Design or identify existing dataset where multiple platforms can be compared on identical recordings; alternatively, request analytical validation data comparing to shared reference

combined_gaps.description: Limited non-English validation for speech endpoints in ALS; articulatory precision cross-language work emerging (correlations 0.66-0.93) but ALS-specific validation in non-English populations limited
combined_gaps.action: Evaluate DOI:10.21437/interspeech.2025-2162 cross-language results; assess whether trial populations requiring non-English endpoints have sufficient validation; NCT07341334 (Spanish) is recruiting but not yet reporting

combined_gaps.description: Winterlight Labs and ki elements lack published longitudinal change-score data specific to ALS in this corpus; their cross-sectional discriminative ability is demonstrated but endpoint suitability for tracking progression is unconfirmed
combined_gaps.action: Request longitudinal datasets or publications from Winterlight Labs and ki elements demonstrating within-patient change detection in ALS; without this, these platforms cannot advance beyond Tier 2 for trial endpoint consideration

combined_gaps.description: Multiple corpus papers use unnamed smartphone applications for speech collection (PMID:41396714, PMID:38837773, PMID:40365751) with validated results but unattributable platforms; these may represent internal-group apps overlapping with named platforms
combined_gaps.action: Contact corresponding authors of key unnamed-app studies to identify platform and assess commercial availability or licensing options

combined_gaps.description: Regulatory clearance status for ki elements and Winterlight Labs platforms not identified in corpus; CE marking or FDA clearance status unknown
combined_gaps.action: Query vendors directly for regulatory status; search FDA 510(k)/De Novo databases and EU EUDAMED for speech analysis medical devices

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Range of Motion (ROM) in ALS encompasses the measurable angular displacement and functional reach envelope of affected body segments. In ALS, progressive motor neuron loss leads to muscle weakness and spasticity that restricts active joint excursion, manifesting as reduced upper extremity reachable workspace, head drop from cervical extensor weakness, and diminished wrist/elbow excursion. The primary operationalized sub-construct with clinical validation is Reachable Workspace Relative Surface Area (RSA), a 3D metric quantifying the proportion of the theoretical arm-length sphere that a patient can actively reach.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: RWS RSA significantly discriminates ALS from healthy controls (0.579 vs 0.786, p<0.001) and correlates with ALSFRS-R UE items (ρ=0.569, p=0.009); narrative review confirms RWS as validated COA in ALS with longitudinal sensitivity (PMID:25965847, PMID:27813421, PMID:41960733)
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: RWS described as a 'valid, reliable, and sensitive UE clinical outcome assessment (COA) tool' with over a decade of clinical study experience, but no specific FDA/EMA endpoint qualification or guidance citation found in corpus; no RCT use as primary/secondary endpoint identified (PMID:41960733)
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Primary validation platform (Microsoft Kinect) is commercially discontinued; methodology is sensor-agnostic per review but no successor depth sensor validated in ALS within this corpus; IMU-based head-neck ROM remains at pilot stage with no specific commercial device named (PMID:41960733, PMID:28068376)

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Reachable Workspace Relative Surface Area (RSA) — proportion of theoretical arm-length hemisphere actively reached, normalized by individual arm length
measurement_spec.epoch: Single bilateral assessment session (5 repeated maximum-reach sweeps per visit); longitudinal assessments at intervals ≥2 months
measurement_spec.algorithm: 3D depth sensor captures joint positions → arm-length normalization → surface area reconstruction of reachable envelope → RSA = patient surface area / maximum possible surface area; quadrant-level decomposition (upper lateral, upper medial, lower lateral, lower medial) available

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Oculomotor function in ALS encompasses measurable eye-movement parameters — saccadic intrusions (square wave jerks), pro-saccade gain and peak velocity, anti-saccade error rate, corrective saccadic latency, smooth pursuit fixation time — that reflect bulbar and frontal-lobe involvement. These measures are extracted from gaze-tracking data during structured visual tasks and correlated with disease severity and progression as indexed by ALSFRS-R total and bulbar subscale scores.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Multiple studies demonstrate oculometric changes specific to ALS that correlate with ALSFRS-R and distinguish patients from healthy controls (PMID:38975625, PMID:37026395)
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: No direct FDA/EMA guidance for oculomotor endpoints in ALS identified in the corpus; exploratory use in a Phase IIb trial is the strongest precedent (PMID:37026395, NCT05407428)
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Software-based oculometric platforms and smartphone eye-tracking apps are commercially available or near-commercial, with demonstrated feasibility in ALS populations (NCT05407428, PMID:39015513)

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Square wave jerk (SWJ) frequency, amplitude, and relative duration during fixation; corrective saccadic latency; pro-saccade gain and peak velocity
measurement_spec.epoch: Not specified in corpus; assessments conducted at multiple timepoints within drug trial duration
measurement_spec.algorithm: Software-based extraction of saccadic intrusions and oculometric parameters from gaze-tracking data during structured visual stimuli (proprietary, NeuraLight)

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Muscle function in ALS encompasses quantitative measurement of voluntary isometric muscle strength and force production across limb muscle groups, reflecting the progressive lower motor neuron degeneration characteristic of ALS. It is operationalized primarily through dynamometry (hand-held, fixed, and grip-specific), with decline in composite or regional strength serving as a direct biomarker of disease progression and a correlate of functional capacity (ALSFRS-R motor domains).

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Muscle strength loss is the cardinal feature of ALS; dynamometry-measured strength is strongly correlated with ALSFRS-R fine and gross motor domains (rs=0.740 and similar magnitudes) in trials with N=148–456 (PMID:42084479, PMID:37641579).
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: Hand-held dynamometry is accepted as a secondary endpoint in multiple Phase 3 ALS RCTs (NCT00349622 N=513; NCT04248465 N=382; NCT06973629 N=200; PMID:36129998 tofersen Phase 3).
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Multiple commercially available dynamometry platforms exist with demonstrated reliability (ICC≥0.97), longitudinal sensitivity, and multi-site deployment in ALS (PMID:28120413, PMID:33355879, PMID:37427714).

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Isometric muscle strength (Newtons or % predicted), composite across multiple muscle groups or individual muscle group scores
measurement_spec.epoch: Per clinic visit (monthly/quarterly) or biweekly-to-daily for home-based devices
measurement_spec.algorithm: Maximum voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC) — peak force over 3–5 second effort, best of 2–3 trials per muscle group
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Protocol-specific; typically requires bilateral testing of ≥4 muscle groups for composite score; single-muscle devices require consistent positioning confirmed by ICC

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Bulbar speech dysfunction in ALS encompasses progressive dysarthria resulting from upper and lower motor neuron degeneration affecting articulatory, phonatory, resonatory, and respiratory speech subsystems. Approximately 25% of people with ALS exhibit bulbar dysfunction at diagnosis, with speech impairment linked to reduced communicative participation, social isolation, and shorter survival. Digital speech measures quantify this decline through acoustic features (articulatory precision, speaking rate, pause patterns, intelligibility) that correlate with clinician-rated severity and standard clinical scales.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Bulbar dysfunction affects 25% at ALS diagnosis and is linked to shorter survival; speech impairment directly measured via ALSFRS-R bulbar subscore and is a primary driver of quality-of-life decline (PMID:38062079, PMID:40726766, PMID:38837773)
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: FDA Breakthrough Device Designation received for articulatory precision measure for monitoring ALS; validated following DiMe V3 framework (PMID:38838248, PMID:38932502)
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Multiple validated platforms exist using standard smartphones/microphones; remote unsupervised collection feasibility demonstrated across >250 participants with high compliance (DOI:10.21437/interspeech.2023-2115, PMID:39126786, DOI:10.1002/acn3.770)

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Articulatory precision (phoneme-level acoustic distance from canonical pronunciation); complementary metrics include speaking rate, timing alignment, and automated listener effort
measurement_spec.epoch: Per speech recording session (reading passage, picture description, or diadochokinesis task); typically collected weekly or biweekly
measurement_spec.algorithm: Phoneme-level acoustic comparison to expected patterns via wav2vec 2.0 or equivalent ASR-derived embeddings; MDC demonstrated lower than MCID (PMID:38932502)
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Not formally standardized across platforms; test-retest reliability established for articulatory precision and DDK rate (ICC 0.92-0.95 reported for select measures)
